title: Special Systems: No Solution and Infinite Solutions

epigraph.text:
There is a way that appears to be right, but in the 
end it leads to death.
epigraph.quote: It is not enough to have a good mind; the main thing is to use it well.

content.text: {It is not enough to have a good mind; the main thing is to use it well.}{Ren\'e Descartes}

Not every system has a single, unique solution. Some systems have no 
solution at all; others have infinitely many. Understanding these 
special cases completes our picture of linear systems.

content.title: Inconsistent System
content.content:
A system of equations is **inconsistent** 
if it has no solution. Graphically, the lines are parallel and never 
intersect.

content.title: Recognizing an inconsistent system
content.solution:
**Using elimination:** Subtract the first equation from the second:
\[
 (2x + 3y) - (2x + 3y) = 12 - 6
\]
\[
 0 = 6 \quad \text{(False!)}
\]

This contradiction means no solution exists.

**Graphically:** Converting to slope-intercept form:
$$
 y &= -\frac{2}{3}x + 2 \\
 y &= -\frac{2}{3}x + 4
$$

Both lines have slope $-\frac{2}{3}$ but different $y$-intercepts. 
They are parallel.

center
tikzpicture[scale=0.5]
 (-1,-1) grid (5,5);
 (-1.5,0) – (5.5,0) node[right] {$x$};
 (0,-1.5) – (0,5.5) node[above] {$y$};
 
 (-0.5,2.33) – (4.5,-1);
 (-0.5,4.33) – (4.5,1);
 
 at (4,0) {$2x+3y=6$};
 at (4,2) {$2x+3y=12$};
tikzpicture
center

The system has **no solution**.

content.title: Dependent System
content.content:
A system is **dependent** if the equations 
represent the same line. Every point on the line is a solution, so there 
are infinitely many solutions.

content.title: Recognizing a dependent system
content.solution:
**Using elimination:** Multiply the first equation by $-3$:
$$
 -3x + 6y &= -12 \\
 3x - 6y &= 12 \\
 \hline
 0 &= 0 \quad \text{(Always true!)}
$$

This identity means the equations describe the same line.

**Verification:** The second equation is exactly $3$ times the first:
\[
 3(x - 2y) = 3(4) \implies 3x - 6y = 12
\]

Converting both to slope-intercept form gives $y = \frac{1}{2}x - 2$.

The system has **infinitely many solutions**. We can express them as:
\[
 \{(x, y) \mid x - 2y = 4\} \quad \text{or} \quad \left\{(x, y) \;\middle|\; y = \frac{1}{2}x - 2\right\}
\]

content.title: Classifying without solving
content.solution:
1. $\dfrac{4}{6} = \dfrac{6}{9} = \dfrac{2}{3}$ and 
 $\dfrac{10}{15} = \dfrac{2}{3}$. All ratios equal. 
 **Dependent** (infinitely many solutions).

2. $\dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and $\dfrac{-5}{-10} = \dfrac{1}{2}$, 
 but $\dfrac{7}{8} \neq \dfrac{1}{2}$.
 Coefficient ratios equal, constant ratios differ. 
 **Inconsistent** (no solution).

3. $\dfrac{2}{5} \neq \dfrac{7}{-2}$. Coefficient ratios differ. 
 **Independent** (one solution).

marginNotes.text:
Small is the gate and narrow the road 
that leads to life.

marginNotes.author: G.H. Hardy
marginNotes.text:
A mathematician, like a painter or a poet, is 
a maker of patterns.
